FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD study, baseline ages 9-10 (n=9,474), data collected 2016-2022, NDA release reference

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis examining prospective relationship between screen time/social media trajectories and anxiety/depression

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time and social media use as IVs paired with anxiety/depression (CBCL) as DV; individual-trait-level mental health outcomes; group-based trajectory models identify distinct screen time and social media patterns associated with significant differences in anxiety/depression t-scores

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple numeric results reported: p-values for group comparisons (p≤0.001, p=0.031, p≤0.014), trajectory groups with group-specific t-score estimates, interaction term analysis for alcohol/e-cigarette moderation

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative inferential analysis: multivariable linear models with group-based trajectory model estimates, t-scores, p-values, and interaction contrasts

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal; however, screen time and social media are IVs in primary analysis (outcome is anxiety/depression), so SM-as-DV-only criterion does not apply. Paper kept.
notes: Paper directly addresses smartphone/social media screen time paired with individual-trait mental health; meets all inclusion criteria with robust quantitative results across multiple analyses

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD cohort, 7,471 young adolescents (mean age 9.87±0.62 years), described as young adolescents from the ABCD study

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using neuroimaging data, transcriptome profiles, PET imaging, and linear mixed effect models to investigate neural correlates of intelligence

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper's primary focus is neural correlates of fluid vs. crystallized intelligence via neuroimaging phenotypes (MRI markers, gene expression, neurotransmitters, PET imaging). Screen use mentioned only as a lifestyle factor in supplementary mediation analysis: "physical activities, screen use and sleep duration influenced fluid intelligence mainly through mGlu5 receptors." Screen use is not the primary IV or DV in a main analysis paired with an individual-trait outcome; it appears as a lifestyle covariate/mediator in exploratory supplementary analyses. FT-IC2 requires qualifying SM/phone variable as IV or DV (not mediator/covariate-only) paired with individual-trait variable in at least one analysis. This paper does not meet that criterion.

notes: Paper examines neural basis of intelligence types with lifestyle factors (including screen use) as supplementary mediators. Screen use does not appear as a primary IV or DV in any main analysis, and the paper's primary focus is not SM/phone-outcome associations but rather neural correlates of intelligence subtypes.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD study, 8,125 participants (mean age 9.93 years at baseline, ages 9-11 at enrollment), 11,875 U.S. adolescents screened

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis examining associations between reading/television viewing and brain morphology and neurocognitive performance with linear mixed effects models and regionwise analyses

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper examines reading books and television viewing (TV-only), not smartphone or social media use. v6 criteria §2.4 explicitly excludes 'TV-only' as not qualifying for FT-IC2. Methods state: 'Daily hours spent reading for pleasure or television viewing were derived from parental questionnaires.' TV is not a qualifying exposure modality per v6 scope (smartphone/SM use only). Paper does not address SM or phone use at any point.

notes: Paper focuses on reading and television habits; does not include smartphone or social media as exposures. Falls outside scope of review which targets SM/phone use exclusively. Reading is not a qualifying screen-based SM/phone modality per v6 criteria.
